basic_info.incident_name_cn: T2实验室反应失控爆炸事故

process_context.operating_conditions: exothermic batch synthesis at ~140°C; cooling water jacket failure during temperature ramp-up

failure_mechanism.root_cause: Inadequate understanding and characterization of the highly exothermic decomposition reaction kinetics and thermal instability of MCMT intermediates.
failure_mechanism.causal_chain: Lack of reactive hazard assessment → uncharacterized decomposition onset at ~130°C → cooling system undersized and failed → reactor temperature exceeded safe limit → thermal runaway → rapid pressure rise → vessel rupture → explosion
failure_mechanism.physical_principles: Thermal runaway driven by self-heating from adiabatic decomposition kinetics (ARC data later showed T_onset=125°C, ΔH_decomp > 1000 J/g)

protection_system.safeguard_failures: Cooling system lacked redundancy and capacity for worst-case heat release; rupture disk was undersized and vented to confined space; no independent critical temperature interlock or emergency quench system.
csb_recommendations: Require comprehensive reactive hazard evaluation (e.g., ARC, DSC) before scale-up
Mandate independent safety review for batch chemical processes with high energy potential
Implement engineered safeguards such as automatic emergency quench or pressure-relief venting to safe location

embedding_text: T2 Laboratories experienced a catastrophic runaway reaction in a jacketed batch reactor synthesizing MCMT. The process involved sodium methoxide, methanol, and manganese metal under elevated temperature (~140°C). A 'More Temperature' deviation occurred due to unrecognized exothermic decomposition onset (~125°C) and inadequate cooling capacity. Root cause was absence of thermal stability testing and kinetic characterization. This led to uncontrollable self-heating, rapid pressure rise, vessel rupture, and explosion—killing four and injuring thirty-two.
